items.q: Why are food chains usually limited to about 4 or 5 trophic levels?
items.correct: Only ~10 percent of energy is transferred between trophic levels; eventually too little energy remains to support another level.
items.misconception: There aren't enough types of animals to fill more trophic levels.
items.random_wrong: Larger animals always eat the next ones up, and there aren't enough giant animals.
items.why: Students don't grasp the energy-loss aspect of food chains; they think the limit is biological diversity rather than thermodynamic efficiency.

items.q: How do decomposers return carbon to the atmosphere?
items.correct: They respire — breaking down dead organic matter and releasing CO2.
items.misconception: Decomposers release carbon by physically breaking matter into smaller pieces.
items.random_wrong: Decomposers turn dead matter into oxygen and water, with no carbon involved.
items.why: Students see decomposition as a physical breakdown rather than a metabolic respiration process happening in microbes and fungi.

items.q: What is the main role of mitochondria in a cell?
items.correct: To carry out aerobic respiration, producing ATP that cells use as an energy currency.
items.misconception: Mitochondria produce energy by creating it from nothing.
items.random_wrong: Mitochondria store sunlight and release it for photosynthesis.
items.why: The phrase 'powerhouse of the cell' makes students think energy is created by mitochondria, missing that they transfer energy from glucose to ATP.

items.q: Is energy recycled around an ecosystem?
items.correct: No — energy flows one way and is lost as heat; only nutrients such as carbon and nitrogen are recycled.
items.misconception: Energy is recycled like carbon and nitrogen are.
items.random_wrong: Energy increases at each step up the food chain.
items.why: Students extend nutrient cycling to energy, but energy enters as sunlight and leaves as heat, so it cannot be reused by the ecosystem.

items.q: Do food chain arrows show which animal eats the other (predator to prey)?
items.correct: No — arrows point from the organism eaten to the one eating it, showing the direction of energy transfer.
items.misconception: Food chain arrows point from the predator to its prey.
items.random_wrong: Arrows point from each organism towards the sun.
items.why: Students read arrows as 'eats', but they show energy flow, which moves from prey to predator (e.g. grass → rabbit → fox).

items.q: Arrows in a food chain point from:
items.why: Correct answer: Eaten to eater. “Eater to eaten” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which process returns CO₂ to the air?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Respiration. “Photosynthesis” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If producers fix 50000 kJ, primary consumers get about (kJ)?
items.why: Exact answer: 5000. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
